perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of the business relationship between the Parties, BROOKS shall, upon written request of CLIENT, deliver any records, data, information and other documents, and all copies thereof, furnished by CLIENT to BROOKS. If CLIENT does not request in writing the return of the above material, BROOKS will retain said material for three (3) years after which BROOKS is free to dispose of material if it so desires and will do so after ten (10) days written notice to CLIENT.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of the business relationship between the Parties, BROOKS shall, upon written request of CLIENT, deliver any records, data, information and other documents, and all copies thereof, furnished by CLIENT to BROOKS. If CLIENT does not request in writing the return of the above material, BROOKS will retain said material for one (1) year after which BROOKS is free to dispose of material if it so desires and will do so after ten (10) days written notice to CLIENT.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the retention period to one year might conflict with statutes of limitations for certain legal claims in New York.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Statute of Limitations for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_explanation: New York CPLR § 213 sets a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract actions. By shortening the retention period to one year, BROOKS might dispose of relevant material before the statute of limitations expires, potentially hindering the CLIENT's ability to pursue a legal claim for breach of contract during the full statutory period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a three-year retention period for client materials, while the changed version reduces it to one year. The Law Explanation states that New York CPLR § 213 sets a six-year statute of limitations for breach of contract actions. Because the one-year retention period is shorter than the six-year statute of limitations, BROOKS could dispose of relevant material before the client's legal claim expires, potentially hindering the client's ability to pursue a legal claim. This creates a legal contradiction because the reduced retention period could prevent the client from exercising their full statutory rights, rendering the changed contractual term unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unavailable, but the Law Explanation provides sufficient information to determine a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted by, and in accordance with, the laws of the State of New York.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed, construed and interpreted by, and in accordance with, the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could affect the enforceability of specific provisions, particularly if they relate to employee rights or confidentiality, where New York law offers stronger protections. It might also affect the convenience and cost of litigation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Choice of Law Rules
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CLS General Obligations Law § 5-1401 dictates the conditions under which New York courts will enforce a choice-of-law provision in a contract. Switching to Delaware law might be challenged if the agreement lacks a reasonable relationship to Delaware or if applying Delaware law would violate a fundamental public policy of New York.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces New York law with Delaware law, which directly contradicts the original agreement's choice of law. NY GOB § 5-1401 allows parties to agree that New York law governs their rights and duties. By switching to Delaware law, the changed text creates a potential contradiction if the agreement lacks a reasonable relationship to Delaware or if applying Delaware law violates a fundamental public policy of New York, as noted in the Law Explanation. The scraped snippets, specifically NY Gen Oblig L § 5-1401, confirm New York's provision for choice of law in contracts exceeding $250,000, which the modification potentially undermines. The change creates a legal contradiction because the enforceability of the contract under Delaware law might be challenged in New York courts if it violates New York public policy.

perturbation.original_text: Any and all notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be furnished in writing, and delivered either by hand delivery, by overnight courier such as Federal Express or by certified or registered U.S. Mail, postage Macintosh HD:Users:vibrant:Downloads:CONFIDENTIALITY AGREEMENT.doc prepaid, return receipt requested, to the intended recipient at such party’s last known business address.
perturbation.changed_text: Any and all notices required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be furnished via email only, to the intended recipient at such party’s last known email address.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring notice only via email may not comply with legal requirements for certain types of official notices in New York, particularly those related to legal proceedings or termination of contracts, which often require documented proof of delivery.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) regarding Service of Process
perturbation.law_explanation: NY CPLR § 2103 specifies the methods for serving papers in civil actions. While email service is permissible under certain circumstances with consent, requiring it as the *only* method might conflict with the statute if a party later denies receipt or if email is not a reliable method in a specific situation, potentially invalidating the notice for legal purposes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content The requested entry could not be found. Try searching OpenLegislation Browse all Laws of New York
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies multiple methods for delivering notices, including options that provide proof of delivery (certified/registered mail, overnight courier). The changed text limits notice to email only, which may not satisfy legal requirements for proof of service in certain legal contexts as specified in NY CPLR § 2103, especially if a party denies receipt. The Law Explanation clearly states that mandating email as the *only* method could conflict with the statute. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates an issue accessing the requested entry, but the Law Explanation still provides sufficient justification for the contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation also confirms that requiring notice only via email may not comply with legal requirements.
